coredata.dc:title: Natural xylose-derived carbon dots towards efficient semi-artificial photosynthesis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zirui
Zhang, Yahui
Zhang, Siyu
Ge, Min
Zhang, Huayang
Wang, Shaobin
Chen, Zhijun
Li, Shujun
Yang, Chenhui
coredata.dc:description: Photosynthesis by plants stores sunlight into chemicals and drives CO2 fixation into sugars with low biomass conversion efficiency due to the unoptimized solar spectrum utilization and various chemical conversion possibilities that follow H2O oxidation. Expanding the solar spectrum utilization and optimizing the charge transfer pathway of photosynthesis is critical to improving the conversion efficiency. Here, a group of carbon dots (CDs) with distinct content of sp2 CC domain are prepared by one-step carbonization of natural xylose, which penetrated natural chloroplasts and integrated with the grana thylakoid to promote in vitro photosynthesis. Structural characterization and electrochemical results reveal the positive impact of graphitization degree on the electron transport capacity of CDs. Classic Hill reaction and ATP production demonstrate the enhanced photosynthetic activity resulting from the CDs-mediated electron transfer of photosystem II. In-depth studies of the structure–function relationship prove the synergistic effect of intensified biotic-abiotic interaction between CDs and chloroplast, lower charge transfer resistance, and extended light absorption. This work posts a promising method to optimize electron transport and improve natural photosynthesis using artificial interventions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photosynthesis
Electron transfer
Hill reaction
Sp2 conjugated structure

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs and photosynthesis mechanism by Chloroplasts/CDs.

Morphology of different CDs and structural characterization of CDs-200: TEM and corresponding high-resolution TEM images of (a, e) CDs-140, (b, f) CDs-160, (c, g) CDs-180 and (d, h) CDs-200; (i) XPS spectrum of CDs-200; (j) High-resolution XPS C 1 s spectra of CDs-200; (k) High-resolution XPS O 1 s spectra of CDs-200.

Characterization of CDs: (a) FT-IR spectra of xylose and different CDs; (b) XRD patterns of different CDs; (c) Raman spectra of different CDs; (d) EIS of different CDs; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of chloroplasts and different CDs, and PL spectrum of CDs-200 (λex = 390 nm).
TEM images of (a) chloroplasts granum, (b) cross-sectional slices of chloroplasts; (c) CDs-200 treated chloroplasts; and (d) cross-sectional slices of CDs-200 treated chloroplasts.

Enhanced photosynthesis and photoelectron production by CDs: Kinetic curves of DCPIP reduction by (a) chloroplasts treated with different CDs Error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3) and (b) Chl/CDs-200 with different concentrations (0–200 μg/mL) of CDs-200 (n = 3) where C represents the absorbance value in the cuvette after different illumination times, and C0 represents the initial absorbance value at 0 min; (c) ATP production by chloroplasts and Chl/CDs-200 hybrid (n = 3), According to the concentration of CDs-200 solution (200 μg/ml), the mass of the introduced CDs was 0, 10, 20, and 40 μg, respectively; (d) ETSA of chloroplasts with/without light irradiation and addition of CDs (n = 3); (e) Photocurrent profiles of chloroplasts with/without addition of CDs-200 over 7 recycles of “on–off” irradiation (tested with open potential and irradiation with Xenon lamp:100 mW/cm2, 10 s on/off). (f) EIS of chloroplasts with/without the addition of different CDs. ‘n’ in Figure a-d represents the biological repetition times.
Schematic diagram of the CDs mediated in vitro photosynthesis.

head.ce:title: Natural xylose-derived carbon dots towards efficient semi-artificial photosynthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials and Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials, The University of Adelaide, Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials, The University of Adelaide, Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chinese Academy of Forestry, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Xiang Shan Road, Haidian, 100091 Beijing China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chinese Academy of Forestry, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Xiang Shan Road, Haidian, 100091 Beijing China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photosynthesis by plants stores sunlight into chemicals and drives CO2 fixation into sugars with low biomass conversion efficiency due to the unoptimized solar spectrum utilization and various chemical conversion possibilities that follow H2O oxidation. Expanding the solar spectrum utilization and optimizing the charge transfer pathway of photosynthesis is critical to improving the conversion efficiency. Here, a group of carbon dots (CDs) with distinct content of sp2 CC domain are prepared by one-step carbonization of natural xylose, which penetrated natural chloroplasts and integrated with the grana thylakoid to promote in vitro photosynthesis. Structural characterization and electrochemical results reveal the positive impact of graphitization degree on the electron transport capacity of CDs. Classic Hill reaction and ATP production demonstrate the enhanced photosynthetic activity resulting from the CDs-mediated electron transfer of photosystem II. In-depth studies of the structure–function relationship prove the synergistic effect of intensified biotic-abiotic interaction between CDs and chloroplast, lower charge transfer resistance, and extended light absorption. This work posts a promising method to optimize electron transport and improve natural photosynthesis using artificial interventions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Already over 3 billion years ago, nature evolved photosynthesis to power all beings on earth by converting solar energy into chemicals and helping maintain the carbon balance of our planet by offsetting anthropogenic emissions of CO2 [1]. Nature photosynthesis has developed mature catalytic machinery in the form of enzymes, many of which exhibit remarkable activity in selectivity, rates, and energy efficiency [2]. The natural matrix that carries out photosynthesis contains light-harvesting complexes (i.e., photosystem II (PSII) and I (PSI)) on chloroplast thylakoids to absorb sunlight and produce energized carriers that execute chemical reactions within the photosystems [3]. Despite the impressive catalytic activities and quantum efficiencies of specific enzymes, natural photosynthesis is inefficient in the overall biological solar-to-biomass conversion rate (<6%) [4]. This is because photosynthetic organisms prioritize optimization of survival strategies under dynamic and stressful environmental conditions over solar-to-biomass conversion efficiencies, resulting in many charge transfer pathways and chemical conversion possibilities. In addition, the unoptimized solar spectrum utilization and inefficient internal metabolic also diminish the conversion efficiency [5].

Improving both light-harvesting capabilities and electron transfer is key to enhancing the natural photosynthesis of plant chloroplasts. Genetic engineering offers a platform to improve biocatalytic efficiency by increasing the expression of oxidoreductases in the photosystems, but this is an expensive and complicated operation [6,7]. Augmented in vivo or vitro photosynthetic performance has been achieved by integrating nanomaterials (e.g., metal nanoclusters and semiconducting materials), polymers (e.g., poly(boron-dipyrromethene-co-fluorene)), and photosensitizer (e.g., molecular dyes) [8–10] with living plant or isolated chloroplast. Inorganic semiconductors (e.g., cadmium sulfide nanoparticles) with superior light absorption and electron/hole separation ability could increase solar energy utilization and conversion in such semi-artificial photosystems [11,12]. Molecular dyes, acting as light harvesters, have been assembled with plant chloroplasts in the form of conductive polymers to construct semi-artificial systems with enhanced photosynthetic performance [13–15]. Such systems, however, require expensive or biotoxic materials, which may last relatively short periods. Hence, environmentally friendly and biocompatible candidates are highly desired to be developed and integrated with natural photosynthesis for solar energy conversion [16,17].

As an emerging nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) show the advantages of excellent conductivity, luminescence performance, good biocompatibility, chemical stability and large specific surface area, and has been widely used in luminescence [18,19], catalytic chemistry [20], biomedicine [21], sensors [22], etc. We previously fed CDs to representative bacteria for bioelectricity generation, proving its great biocompatibility [23]. It has comparable or even better physicochemical properties to traditional semiconductors in the characteristics such as light absorption, fluorescence properties, and conductivity. Again, its abundant raw materials, facile synthesis, and versatility make it an ideal substitute for traditional organic/inorganic counterparts in various photochemical applications [24,25].

Based on these benefits, carbon dots with high electron transport capacity have been attempted to promote natural photosynthesis [26,27]. Substantial strides have recently been made toward improving the light-harvesting efficiency in CDs-containing semi-artificial photosystems by regulating the complementary light absorption between the CDs and the plant chloroplasts [28–32]. Of particular interest is the excellent electron acceptor and donator, which efficiently accelerates efficient biotic-abiotic interfacial electron transfer in photosynthesis [15,33]. So far, most CDs promote photosynthesis by adjusting the emission spectrum of CDs to match the absorption spectrum of the hybrid system [28,34]. Nevertheless, how the internal structure of CDs promotes photosynthesis, especially how sp2 CC conjugated structure in the disordered region of CDs affects the electron transfer rate of PSII, is rarely studied.

As a natural monosaccharide, xylose is easy to obtain and can be carbonized to synthesize carbons. Here, four CDs (CDs-140, CDs-160, CDs-180, and CDs-200), with distinct microstructures were prepared via one-step hydrothermal carbonization of xylose at different temperatures (140 ℃, 160 ℃, 180 ℃, and 200 ℃) (Fig. 1 ). We propose a strategy to boost photosynthesis using xylose-derived CDs with high electron transportability (Fig. 1). The structural and electrochemical characteristics of the CDs were systematically investigated using a variety of analytical techniques. Interestingly, as the carbonization temperature increases, the disorder degree of CDs increases, and so does the ratio of sp2 CC domain. The investigation of the structural-activity relationship proved the favorable effects of the disordered structure and sp2 CC states of CDs on the biotic-abiotic interfacial electron transfer and photosynthesis efficiency. By integrating the engineered CDs with natural photosynthesis, this work establishes a proof of concept semi-artificial photosystem for solar energy conversion.

Typically, a mixture of deionized water (20 mL), xylose (200 mg) and HCl (800 μL) was placed in a 50 mL tetrafluoroethylene container and heated to different temperatures (140 ℃, 160 ℃, 180 ℃, and 200 ℃, respectively) for 6 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was evaporated by rotary evaporation and then freeze-dried to obtain the CDs powders. The CDs were labeled CDs-140, CDs-160, CDs-180, and CDs-200 according to different hydrothermal temperatures. In the above steps, adding HCl is a carbonation-promoting operation and has been verified to completely promote the carbonization of xylose [35]. The filter membrane is to filter larger particle impurities.

The morphology of the different CDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), using a JEM-2100F field emission electron microscope (JEOL ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The composition and chemical structure were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using a Nicolet Is5 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific., Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific., Waltham, MA, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution (Horiba ltd., Kyoto, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1901 double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Persee General Instrument ltd., Beijing, China). Photoluminescence properties of the different CDs were determined using an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK). Cross-section slices of chloroplasts and granum (a stacked membranous structure within the chloroplasts) with/without the addition of CDs were characterized by TEM (Hitachi ltd., Tokyo, Japan).

Sucrose buffer solution (pH = 7.3) containing sucrose (0.4 mol/L), KCl (0.01 mol/L), Na2HPO4 (0.03 mol/L) and KH2PO4 (0.02 mol/L) was used to extract chloroplasts from fresh spinach. The chloroplast-containing mixture was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 3 min to obtain a chloroplast-containing precipitate, which was re-dispersed in sucrose buffer. All of the above operations were performed at 0–4 ℃ in the dark. The concentration was determined using the formula after dispersing the chloroplasts in a mixture of acetone and water (8:2, v/v) and measuring absorbance at 652 nm [36].

ETSA was measured under dark and light irradiation (Xenon lamp: 100 mW cm−2) conditions, with 2,3,5-triphenyl-2H-tetrazolium chloride (TTC) as the electron acceptor and acetone as the extractant, following our previously reported methods [23]. Briefly, Tris-HCl buffer (1 mL), Na2SO3 (0.36 %, 0.5 mL) and TTC (0.4 %, 1 mL) were added to the mixture of chloroplast suspension (20 μg/mL, 3 mL), and solution of CDs-200 (200 μg/mL). After 10 min treatment (with/without light), formaldehyde (37 %, 1 mL) was added to terminate the reaction, and the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm. The resulting residue was re-dispersed in acetone (3 mL) and the OD485 was measured by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. A standard curve was constructed using the same method, except that Na2S2O4 was used instead of chloroplasts. ETSA was calculated using the following equation:
ETSA =
OD
485
× V
k × w × t
where OD 485 is the absorbance of the sample, V is the volume of acetone (3 mL), k is the slope of the standard curve, w is the weight of chloroplasts and t is the irradiation time of the chloroplasts (10 min).

Photosynthetic activity was determined using the Hill reaction with 2,6-dichlorophenolindophenol (DCPIP) as the electron acceptor. Detailly, 200 μL of DCPIP (60 μM) was added to the mixture of CDs (CDs-140, CDs-160, CDs-180 or CDs-200) and chloroplast suspension (20 μg/mL, 5 mL) after incubation. Then 3 mL of the above-mixed solution was transferred to a cuvette with a closed screw cap and irradiated with simulated sunlight (Xenon lamp:100 mW cm−2) for 10 min. No obvious sediment was observed in the solution during the irradiation. The solution for the Hill reaction consisted mainly of chloroplast-derived sucrose buffer and a small amount of water from DCPIP and CDs. Therefore, the presence of even a small amount of uncarbonated xylose derived from CDs is inconsequential compared to the large amount of carbohydrates in the Hill reaction solvent system. Every minute, the cuvette was inserted into the UV–vis spectrometer to measure the absorbance of the mixture at 600 nm (OD600) to evaluate the photosynthesis process [37]. The following equation was used to show the change of absorbance (Δ Abs) over time, C represents the absorbance value in the cuvette after different illumination times, and C0 represents the initial absorbance value at 0 min.
Δ A b s =
C 0
- C

Chloroplasts stock solution were mixed with CDs suspension (200 μg/mL; 0, 50, 100, or 200 μL), incubated, and then irradiated (Xenon lamp:100 mW/cm2) for 10 min. In the presence of lysates, ATP was extracted from the mixture by ultrasound, which was then measured using a bioluminescent ATP kit (Beyotime Biotechnology) and multifunctional enzyme markers, respectively. All operations were carried out at 4 °C [38].

Both photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance tests were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Shanghai CH Instruments Co., ltd). A three-electrode configuration was employed with the Ag/AgCl and Pt as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. For the working electrode, indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass (1 × 2 cm2) was successively treated with acetone, ethanol, and water. The sample solutions including chloroplast suspension (20 μg/mL, 600 μL), CDs solution (200 μg/mL, 200 μL) and Nafion (5 % w/w in water and 1-propanol, 100 μL). Nafion D-520 dispersion was mixed with the sample solution and used to immobilize the samples onto the ITO. The test sample slurry (200 μL) was then drop coated on the surface of ITO. The frequency of the impedance test ranged from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The photocurrent as a function of time was captured under the periodic simulated solar light irradiation (Xenon lamp: 100 mW/cm2, 10 s on/off). Na2SO4 (0.5 M) solution was used as the electrolyte in the above tests.

As shown in Fig. 2 a–d, all CDs (-140, −160, −180, and −200) were homogeneously dispersed, with average diameters of 1.90 ± 0.01, 2.09 ± 0.003, 3.5 ± 0.04 and 3.2 ± 0.07 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2e–h) showed obvious crystalline structures of the CDs, with similar lattice distances (∼0.21 nm), corresponding to the interplanar distance of the graphitic carbon (100) plane [39,40]. Noteworthily, CDs-140 was obtained in low yield and showed dimensional non-uniformity, probably due to the incomplete carbonization of the xylose.

XPS and FT-IR analyzed the composition and structure of the CDs. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 2i, S2a, S3a and S4a) demonstrates the coexistence of C and O elements for all CDs. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2i) of the CDs-200 by split-peak fitting revealed three forms, sp2 (CC, 283.2 eV), sp3 (CC, 284.7 eV), together with a sub-peak (CO/C-OH, 287.2 eV) [41,42]. In the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra, the binding energy of sp2 is lower than usual, which is attributed to the existence of the electron-withdrawing groups of CDs (defects, pentagons, hexagons, and hydrogen, etc.) [43]. The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2k) showed two types of bonds, CO (530.3 eV) and C-OH (531.5 eV) [44,45]. In addition, the XPS spectra of CDs-140, CDs-160 and CDs-180 were shown in Fig S2-S4. In order to show the changes in the internal structure of CDs with temperature increase, the ratio of CC to CC integral area was used to examine the internal structural changes of the four CDs, which were calculated to be 0.52, 0.89, 0.95, and 1.01, respectively (Table S1). The ratio of sp2 carbon increased with the elevated synthesis temperature due to the dehydration intensification of xylose at higher temperatures [35].

The XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a) showed broad 2θ peaks at ∼ 20°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, confirming the graphite-like carbon structure of the CDs [44,46]. The FT-IR spectra of all the CDs displayed similar stretching vibrations (Fig. 3b). With xylose as a reference, the spectra of all the CDs showed broad stretching vibrations at 3000–3550 cm−1, which were assigned to the surface hydroxyl groups [47,48]. Changes in intensity of the intense stretching vibration at ∼ 1700 cm−1 (CO) and the peak at 1050 cm−1 (COC) were attributed to intensified dehydration degree of the xylose [49,50].

The Raman spectra showed distinct bands at ∼ 1340 cm−1 and ∼ 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively [51]. The D band is considered to represent a disordered sp2 hybridized carbon structure, while the G band represents crystalline graphitization [52]. ID/IG values were calculated to be 0.74, 0.91, 0.98 and 0.99 for CDs-140, CDs-160, CDs-180 and CDs-200, respectively (Fig. 3c) [53]. This result indicates that more disordered structures are generated with increasing reaction temperature. The molecular structure of xylose precursor is rich in O atoms, which may cause the formation of defects during the carbonization process. During the synthesis of CDs, with the increase of hydrothermal temperature, intermolecular dehydration of xylose becomes more severe, leading to the rise of defect states, manifested as the high D value [54].

We then applied electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to evaluate the electron transport capacity at CDs /electrolyte interfaces. The semicircle diameters in low-frequency regions are related to interfacial charge transfer resistance (R ct), which is critical in revealing the interfacial properties. It was evidenced that the semicircle diameters (R ct) of the CDs-based electrodes decreased in the order CDs-200 < CDs-180 < CDs-160 < CDs-140 (Fig. 3d), indicating more rapid charge transfer from the electrode to electrolyte was achieved for the CDs synthesized with higher temperature [55]. The lower electron transfer resistances are due to the gradual increase of the sp2 CC content.

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The absorption peaks at 230 nm in the UV–vis spectra are indicative of π → π* electron transitions of aromatic structures, and the peaks at 280 nm were attributed to n → π* electron transitions of the surface state (Fig. 3e) [56]. We found that both π → π* and n → π* electronic transitions of four CDs displayed an increasing trend because of more sp2 conjugated structure caused by increased carbonization at a higher temperature. The PL spectra of all CDs showed strong and bright fluorescence emission at ∼ 600 nm (Fig. 3e, S5–8). The fluorescence spectrum of CDs has a wider half-peak width, which may be caused by the non-uniform particle size distribution, rather than the impurity of the CDs. And the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs is mainly located in the visible region, which is consistent with the light absorption spectrum of plant photosynthesis. The spectral overlap between the PL emission of the CDs and the absorption of chloroplasts indicates that the CDs have the potential to boost photosynthesis.

The main advantage of CDs in promoting photosynthesis is that CDs own excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. In addition, the CDs do not contain elements such as N, P, and S, and will not cause eutrophication and other pollution to the ecological environment. We then investigated their biocompatibility through a cell viability test. As shown in Fig. S9, human umbilical vein endothelial cells retained excellent viability after 48 h incubation with CDs at concentrations of up to 800 μg/mL. TEM was used to determine the distribution of CDs in the chloroplasts. As shown in Fig. 4 a and c, a relatively rough granum surface was formed after CD incubation for 12 h, markedly different from the pristine chloroplasts. Furthermore, noticeable speckles were observed in the cross-sectional slices of granum (Fig. 4b, d), indicating CDs could penetrate and enter the inside of the chloroplast structure. This also revealed that CDs are mainly distributed on the surface of the stacked thylakoid and granums. Attributing to the nanoscale size, CDs can easily traverse the biological barriers, including cell membranes and the low biotoxicity of carbon-based inorganic nanomaterials does not affect the normal metabolism in the organism [57,58]. These results illustrated the effective integration and dispersion of CDs on chloroplasts.

The Hill reaction is an efficient way to evaluate the in vitro activity of photosystem II (PSII) [59]. The blue dye dichlorophenol indophenol (DCPIP) is commonly used as an electron acceptor in the Hill reaction. The decoloration of the DCPIP during reduction can indicate the number of electrons extracted from photosynthesis. Here, DCPIP (60 μmol/L) was added to a mixture of chloroplasts with CDs-140, CDs-160, CDs-180 and CDs-200, then irradiated with simulated solar light (100 mW/cm2). The absorbance at 600 nm was used to quantify the progress of the reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 b, with the extension of irradiation, the reaction systems showed gradually decreased absorbance at 600 nm due to the reduction of DCPIP. Compared with the bare chloroplast, the relative absorbance of the hybrids (Chl/CDs-140, Chl/CDs-160, Chl/CDs-180, and Chl/CDs-200) demonstrated a more noticeable decrease, indicating the enhanced photoreduction ability of the hybrids (Fig. 5a). Since the curve shows a linear trend within 4 min, the absorbance changes as a function of time within this period were used to estimate the photosynthetic rate. As a result, the corresponding photosynthetic rates increased from 0.070 min−1 (Chl.) to 0.142 min−1, 0.152 min−1, 0.168 min−1, and 0.180 min−1 for Chl/CDs-140, Chl/CDs-160, Chl/CDs-180, and Chl/CDs-200, respectively. And the promotion effect of CDs on photosynthesis is significantly enhanced when the synthesis temperature increases. We further conducted parallel experiments to understand the individual structure–function relationship of CDs on the improvement of in vitro photosynthetic efficiency. As shown in Fig. S10, no Hill reaction occurred in the absence of chloroplasts, excluding the reduction activity of CDs on DCPIP even under light irradiation. The photosynthesis rate was raised with the increase of concentration of chloroplast. As the light intensity was reduced, the Hill reaction rate showed a slowing trend (Fig. S11). CDs-200 were selected to measure the effect of CDs concentration on the reduction rate of DCPIP. The reaction rate accelerated with increasing concentration of CDs-200. In order to verify the mechanism of CDs promoting photosynthesis, CDs-200 was used as the sample in the following experiments.

An increase in the levels of ATP, the energy source for use and storage at a cellular level, is the most direct proof of enhanced photosynthesis by PSII. As shown in Fig. 5c, the ATP production from chloroplasts was measured to be 0.098 nmol/mL. Notably, with the introduction of different amounts of CDs (10 to 40 μg) into the system, ATP output was increased to 0.117 nmol/mL, 0.232 nmol/mL, and 0.287 nmol/mL, respectively. After CDs incubation, the electrons extracted by PSII can be involved in the subsequent cascade photosynthesis more quickly, resulting in more efficient ATP production by the ATP synthase. Electron transport system activity (ETSA) was measured to evaluate the photogenerated electron capacity and electron transfer ability of the hybrids under dark and light irradiation (Fig. 5d). Higher ETSA was observed on the Chl/CDs hybrid under light irradiation, which increased the activity by 33 % compared with that of pristine chloroplasts (0.092 vs 0.069 μg μg−1h−1). The enhancement correlates well with the efficient electron transfer mediated by CDs. Enhancement of photocurrent is also essential to verify improved photoelectric response capabilities of the hybrid. The photocurrent of Chl/CDs-200 was significantly higher than that of pristine chloroplasts (Fig. 5e), which gives strong evidence for the involvement of the CDs in the electron transfer process [60]. EIS is regarded as an important method for determining the electron transfer resistance (R ct) of catalysts [61]. CDs treated Chloroplasts indicated a lower R ct than pristine chloroplasts (Fig. 5f), and the Nyquist radius decreased with the order of Chl/CDs-200 < Chl/CDs-180 < Chl/CDs-160 < Chl/CDs-140 < Chl. [62,63]. Although the ratio of disorder structure increases, the internal resistance decreases due to the synergistic effect introduced by the embeddedness between the ordered graphitized structure and disordered carbon [64]. In addition, we briefly summarize several methods for promoting photosynthesis reported in the literature (Table S2). Among them, carbon dots are one of the most environmentally friendly and economical ways to promote.

Considering all the robust properties described above, we propose the following mechanism (illustrated in Fig. 6 ) for the enhancement of photosynthesis of chloroplast by CDs: i) the tight anchoring of CDs to thylakoid membrane allows efficient biotic-abiotic interfacial communication [65]; ii) the spectral overlap between CDs and chloroplasts enables the CDs to extend the light-harvesting range of the chloroplasts and thus improve the utilization of sunlight [35,66]; iii) The optimized CDs could promote the transfer of electrons derived from PSII through the synergistic effect between graphited and amorphous carbon structure [67].

Xylose-derived carbon dots with the specific content of sp2 CC domain were prepared by changing hydrothermal synthesis temperature and anchored to isolated chloroplasts to construct a hybrid photosynthesis system. The variation of synthesis temperature affected the graphitization degree of the carbon structures, directly affecting the physicochemical properties of carbon dots. Lower charge transfer resistance and complementary light absorption could be hence achieved on the CDs/chloroplasts hybrids, which were crucial to photosynthesis performance improvement. The enhanced ETSA and photoelectric response showed that the CDs successfully promote electron transfer at this biotic/abiotic interface. In addition, the Hill reaction and ATP production demonstrate the positive mediation of CDs for the cascade reactions occurring on thylakoid membranes. This work provides a new perspective for applying synthetic nanomaterials to enhance natural photosynthesis. The mechanism of efficiently promoting the photosynthesis of chloroplasts has gradually become apparent, but how to enable it in a non-toxic, harmless and efficient manner remains to be studied. To realise the practical application, a large amount of CDs is needed to promote the photosynthesis of crops on a broad land, and achieving the scalable synthesis of CDs with a low cost is still a challenge. In addition, studies on the effects of CDs on plant genes are scarce, which is still a huge challenge for researchers.

Zirui Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yahui Zhang: Resources, Conceptualization. Siyu Zhang: Data curation. Min Ge: Data curation. Huayang Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shaobin Wang: Methodology, Resources. Zhijun Chen: Methodology, Resources. Shujun Li: Methodology, Resources. Chenhui Yang: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M690572). H. Zhang and S. Wang acknowledge the partial support from the Australian Research Council (DP200101105).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.09.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evidence for oxygenic photosynthesis half a billion years before the Great Oxidation Event

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. J. Planavsky, D. Asael, A. Hofmann, C. T. Reinhard, S. V. Lalonde, A. Knudsen, X. L. Wang, F. O. Ossa, E. Pecoits, J. B. Smith, N. J. Beukes, A. Bekker, T. M. Johnson, K. O. Konhauser, T. W. Lyons, O. J. Rouxel, Evidence for oxygenic photosynthesis half a billion years before the Great Oxidation Event. Nat. Geosci. 7 (2014) 283–286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzymes and bio-inspired electrocatalysts in solar fuel devices
